node.text:
The pendulum wave is a classic physics demo: many pendulums with slightly different lengths are released together. They start in phase, then drift in and out of phase, and a sine wave appears to travel along the row. After one full cycle of the longest pendulum, they realign. This simulation shows exactly that, with real physics and adjustable parameters.
Each pendulum obeys simple harmonic motion. Its period is set by length and gravity: T = 2π√(L/g). So longer strings have longer periods. Here, the lengths are chosen so that in a fixed “cycle time” (e.g. 40 seconds), pendulum 1 completes 51 oscillations, pendulum 2 completes 52, and so on. After that time, they are all in phase again and the wave pattern repeats. The angle at time t is θ(t) = θ₀ cos(ωt) e^(-γt) for small angles, with ω = 2π/T. Damping (γ) makes the amplitude decay over time.
You can change pendulum count, cycle time, damping, time scale, and initial angle. Increasing the count gives a finer wave; shortening the cycle time makes the pattern repeat faster. The decorative wave in the bottom panel reflects the same harmonic motion.
Try the full interactive simulation: https://simulateitnow.com/
#PendulumWave #Physics #SimpleHarmonicMotion #ClassicalMechanics #ScienceEducation #Simulation #T2piRootLoverG #WavePhysics #Oscillations #STEM #PhysicsDemo #Education #Visualization #Interactive #Learning #Science
Try this simulation yourself at simulateitnow.com
